name: Jimmu
description: Emperor Jimmu, traditionally considered the first Emperor of Japan, holds a pivotal position in Japanese mythology and national identity. While historical evidence supporting his existence is debated among scholars, his legendary narrative serves as a cornerstone of the imperial lineage and the creation of the Japanese nation. According to the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, the earliest written records of Japanese history, Jimmu was a descendant of the sun goddess Amaterasu, sent to establish a kingdom in the Yamato region. His eastward expedition from Hyuga, marked by divine assistance and strategic alliances, culminates in his enthronement at Kashihara, marking the beginning of the imperial dynasty. Jimmu's reign is characterized by the consolidation of power, the suppression of local deities and tribes, and the establishment of a centralized political structure. He embodies the divine mandate of the imperial family and the unification of Japan under a single ruler. His story is thus not merely a historical account but a powerful mythological narrative that legitimizes the imperial institution and shapes national consciousness.
subtitle: First Emperor of Japan

keyMyths.title: The Eastern Expedition
keyMyths.description: Jimmu's legendary eastward expedition from Hyuga is a central myth in his narrative. Guided by the Yatagarasu, a three-legged crow sent by Amaterasu, Jimmu and his forces faced numerous challenges, including hostile tribes and treacherous terrain. Through strategic alliances and divine intervention, Jimmu overcame these obstacles, gradually expanding his territory and consolidating his power. This journey symbolizes the arduous process of nation-building and the divine support granted to the imperial lineage, emphasizing the legitimacy of their rule and their divinely ordained right to govern.

keyMyths.title: Enthronement at Kashihara
keyMyths.description: After successfully conquering the Yamato region, Jimmu ascended the throne at Kashihara, marking the official beginning of his reign and the establishment of the Japanese imperial dynasty. This event is portrayed as a pivotal moment in Japanese history, signifying the unification of the country under a single ruler and the establishment of a centralized political structure. Jimmu's enthronement is a symbolic act of claiming divine authority and solidifying the imperial family's position as the rightful rulers of Japan.

keyMyths.title: The Golden Kite
keyMyths.description: During a crucial battle, Jimmu was aided by a golden kite that perched atop his bow, emitting a blinding light that overwhelmed his enemies. This divine intervention secured his victory and solidified his reputation as a divinely protected ruler. The golden kite became a symbol of Jimmu's divine mandate and the supernatural forces that supported his reign. This myth reinforces the belief in the emperor's connection to the celestial realm and the divine favor bestowed upon the imperial line, further legitimizing his rule.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: A complete English translation of the Kojiki, providing valuable insights into the mythology and early history of Japan.
